steps.label: cause
steps.text: Grape spirit (neutral spirit high in alcohol) is added to a partially fermented grape must or wine

steps.label: mechanism
steps.text: The addition raises the alcohol content to a level (typically 15–18% ABV) at which yeast stops; yeast can no longer survive, fermentation halts and unfermented sugar remains in solution

steps.label: effect
steps.text: The finished wine retains residual sugar from the unfermented must, producing a sweet or medium-sweet style

steps.label: exam_formulation
steps.text: Fortification raises alcohol to a level at which yeast stops fermenting; fermentation ceases, retaining residual sugar in the finished wine.

forced_causal_chains.sat_relevance: Use when assessing sweetness in fortified wines. Residual sugar in Port or sweet Sherry results directly from the timing of fortification — earlier addition means sweeter result.

steps.label: cause
steps.text: Mechanical harvesting can break berries before they reach the winery.

steps.label: mechanism
steps.text: When berries are broken, the must is exposed to oxygen before processing, which can begin premature oxidation of aromas and increase phenolic extraction.

steps.label: effect
steps.text: The finished wine can show reduced aromatic freshness and potentially greater extraction of phenolic compounds compared with carefully handled intact fruit.

steps.label: exam_formulation
steps.text: Mechanical harvesting can reduce aromatic freshness because berry breakage exposes must to oxygen before winemaking; this increases oxidation risk unless handling is rapid and protective.
